topic: Individuals, Firms, Markets and Market Failure
subject: economics

items.correct: A cost imposed on a third party not involved in the transaction — e.g. pollution from a factory affects neighbours who didn't choose to bear it.
items.misconception: A negative externality is something bad that happens to a business.
items.random_wrong: A negative externality is a tax on a business.
items.why: The 'externality' is external to the market participants — borne by THIRD parties. Students miss the structure: producer, consumer, then someone else who didn't agree to be affected.

items.q: What is asymmetric information as a source of market failure?
items.correct: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — leading to problems like adverse selection (used-car market) or moral hazard (insurance). Markets can fail or unravel when information is unequal.
items.misconception: Asymmetric information means a buyer and seller disagree about a price.
items.random_wrong: Asymmetric information is when a market only operates in one direction.
items.why: Disagreement about value is normal trade. Asymmetric information is about one side genuinely knowing more — Akerlof's 'lemons' problem shows how this can destroy entire markets. Students collapse the concept into ordinary haggling.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.
The answer examiners want is: Asymmetric information. “Pollution” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Goods underconsumed due to positive externalities are:
items.why: Correct answer: Merit goods. “Public goods” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Akerlof publish 'The Market for Lemons'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1970 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
